sentences.text: Yeah.
My full name is Gabriel Krause.
Yeah.
G-A-B-R-I-E-- --E-L, Gabriel.
Krause, K-R-A-U-S-E.
That's it.
I am born in Warsaw, 16 of August, 1912.
Yeah.
In Polish it is the same like in Yiddish, Gabriel, 'Gah-briel.'
Krause.
No, the last name in Polish is no S.
It is Z.
Because S sound German, and down there, they don't want to hear German names.
I grew up in a Warsaw.
And I went to the school in Warsaw.
Yeah.
The Polish-- the city of Warsaw was a center of Jewish culture and everything, what is connected with culture and education is more connected with Warsaw.
Because you got a lot of Yeshivas, and a lot of Hebrew schools.
They're named [POLISH].
And they got a lot of Jewish newspapers from left to extra to right.
So you got maybe a half a dozen newspapers in Jewish.
In one paper, it was a special, good paper in the Polish language, called [POLISH].
The news in the paper was news in all politically social affairs going on in Poland, and all around the world.
This was a Jewish newspaper especially for intelligentsia, what they can read, Jewish, they start to be assimilated.
And they read just not [INAUDIBLE].
In your home, your father read.
He was-- he was--
And my father, he, in Warsaw, read one paper.
They call it [INAUDIBLE].
[INAUDIBLE].
And it in Jewish.
It's-- it's in just Yiddish, yeah.
Jewish, or Yiddish, yeah.
I read the Polish and Jewish papers.
And that's just my education.
Mostly I read Polish.
The relation between Jews and Poles, it was sometimes very bad, and sometimes between.
Mostly they don't like the Jews, now they have no choice.
They tolerate you.
So the Jews, in fact, they have no choice.
They was by nationality, Jews.
And they wasn't Polish.
They are not Polacks.
And they-- and they live in-- mostly they live like, between not Polish and not Jewish.
They have no other country to go, Polish-- in Poland.
So Poland was really a-- a place for the Jews living kind of [INAUDIBLE], in their last 10 years before the second war.
Yeah.
In my family, my father got a business, what he inherited or bought from my grandfather.
And it was located in center of city and-- and and the street, what I remember, it was [INAUDIBLE].
This is all big [INAUDIBLE] I can see from one end of Warsaw to another.
And we was in the center, and he got the business-- a hardware business.
That's how we making a living, from this hardware business.
Wolf.
Wolf, yeah.
W-O-L-F, Wolf.
My mother's name was Esther, [?
Esthera. ?] Or they call her sometime [?
Justine, ?] in English, [?
Justine. ?] Now they call her, in Poland, [?
Esthera, ?] [?
Estherka ?] or Esther.
Yeah.
Yeah, I got six brothers.
Yeah.
One, the older brother from me was Jacob.
Then next me, Gabriel.
After Gabriel was David.
After David was Misha, Mike, Mordehai, I mean.
And after Mordehai was Henrik.
And after Henrik was Severin.
And this is us, six brothers.
And from six brothers, left one, Misha.
Mike, they call him Mike here.
And he's here.
Yeah, the address I remember, where I live.
We live on [INAUDIBLE] 89.
This is the corner of [INAUDIBLE] Street, yeah.
And we was one family in the whole house the origin-- of-- of Jewish origin.
You can understand this, if we feel not so good about this.
Well, I mean, they-- they see a Jew, and they hate him, and they can't do nothing about this.
That's it.
I believe, over there.
The neighborhood mostly was Polish.
And we was in the Polish neighborhood.
And it there was a very small percentage of Jewish in this-- in this neighbor.
Yeah, so-- so I live with the Polacks.
I live with the Polish culture.
I learn the language from the beginning in Poland, because I go to-- I went to gymnasium, the Polish gymnasium.
And I finish the Polish gymnasium.
In fact, the Jewish was the secondary language and Polish was my first language, in fact.
Because I was-- I must converse, always, in Polish between-- in school, in university, or with Polacks anyway.
Yeah.
Not elementary.
I went to the first class, and I was prepared to first class through the eighth class.
Eighth class, you finish, you must have a [POLISH], it's Polish, [POLISH], eighth class.
When you finish [POLISH], when you finish with regular grades, you got a-- you got the chance to go to university.
You know, because you a Jew--
Yes, because of a Jew, you got the chance to be one in a thousand to find a way to university.
So Jews, mostly all, they-- they emigrate to Belgium or France, and Germany, when they can.
And they finish over there all the specialty schools.
This was years 1935, '36, '37, '38 and '39.
And the Jews was-- there was-- I mean, you cannot go through a university in Poland.
It was very hard.
Why?
Because you're Jewish.
It was a special law.
And the lawyers, [LATIN], in-- in Latin, no Jews at all.
You got the chance to go to university, sit down in the back and hear what they're talking about.
And that's all.
They don't give you-- very seldom.
It's you must be very bright or must have very good connections to go to university in Poland.
Yeah, I belong--
Yeah, I belong to organization.
It's called Maccabi.
It was a Jewish sports organization.
I would go over there once a week or twice a week.
And we learned swimming and other type of sports, like fencing and running and-- and boating, and something, what we got over there.
Everybody-- everybody choose something, what they like it.
So I like swimming and boating is what's good for me.
Or-- and there was other activities.
It was sports, connected with sports.
And there was the organization got this special place on the river Vistula.
The Vistula was the main river in-- in-- in Poland.
And we got a special-built place.
And people give donations, something.
They built like, a pool inside the water of Vistula, that we can swim inside or swim outside.
And we can do other things in sports.
I mean, jumping and something like this.
Boxing, and boxing too, yeah.
No, girls too.
Girls do something else.
They don't box, not with us, anyway.
[LAUGHTER]
So the girls, there were all kind of other activities, swimming and dancing or flirting.
[LAUGHTER] It's one of the activities, I mean-- [LAUGHS]
We experienced it every day.
Antisemitism was-- it was really-- you feel this in the air.
Because the atmosphere in-- in Poland was-- it's-- was like, you feel this in the air, there's something, they don't like us.
They need us.
They don't want us.
So the Jews, the Jews do the best what they can to adjust to this situation.
Because we don't have other way to go.
We don't have a land.
We're just Jews in a Polish land.
And not-- we're not citizen 100%.
We just-- we're just below.
A Jew cannot be a janitor.
A Jew cannot be a postman.
A Jew cannot be-- he can't be nothing.
He just-- he cannot be officer in the army, very seldom.

So-- And the Jews was in Warsaw, special-- not just in Warsaw, in all Poland-- they was in-- in-- in small industries, home industries, or rich people, what they got big factories in Polish, they don't know who is the one that they-- they-- that's worked this way, yeah.
Yeah, there was episodes.
In 1938, it was-- in Poland there was this big organization.
They call it [INAUDIBLE], [POLISH].
And they could-- their state-- this-- this kind of organization was very antisemitic.
They don't want Jews.
They want the Jews out.
Some way, they want out.
And the propaganda go on, we're supposed to get rid of the Jews, some way.
Other way by pogroms, small pogroms in towns, or in the big cities.
In the 1938, I remember, '37, '38, was in some places, it was dangerous to a Jew to show up anyway.
Because the Polack--
The Polack can recognize a Jew by the eyes.
By the eyes, you see?
Or by fixture of the face.
And they don't like the Jews.
And they attack the Jews.
And this-- and this-- and-- [INAUDIBLE] organization got connections.
Some way between Germany, they give them the money, in fact, to make this-- this-- this-- this-- this-- this boiling pot in Poland between these minorities.
Because there was a lot of minorities in Poland-- Poland.
Oh, I got experience.
We-- we go out in the city sometime, we go out.
We go in threes, three people.
One to keep a-- a lit pipe and two people stay on the side way.
Because this way we were saved.
If somebody attack us, they can give him with the lit pipe over the head.
And they run away.
Then they know the-- the Jews doesn't-- doesn't go for this, they'll be killed over nothing or abused for nothing.
That's how, then, we-- we do this.
And we try to help our-- ourselves.
No this is not-- wasn't a solution for a long then anyway.
Our organization, I belong to organization.
They call it Betar, [INAUDIBLE].
And-- and we try because we see the future of the Jews in Poland wasn't good anyway.
There's no way out.
And no-- no way out anyway.
Because the restriction of immigration was all over-- all over Europe.
We cannot go no place.
So we tried to do by unlegal legal immigration to Israel, some way.
And we organize some way. i remember we organize a-- a walk to Israel, with our organization, a walk.
This year was in 1937, I think.
And it was organized, a couple hundred, maybe thousands of our organization, people rather walking to the border of Romania to go to Israel.
This was a type of working for propaganda mostly, too.
The world-- the world looked-- the whole world, they see what we, in some conditions, we would have no way out.
So we tried this way.
No, this is not the way out anyway.
Maybe a thousand or more.
I wasn't there because I was in other things.
I don't walk over there, no.
My family?
No, they cannot walk.
No, no.
This is just special people.
They-- they choose some special people that can endure it and things like this.
Not everybody can do it.
So-- so I got something to it, you know, something else anyway.
My father and mother, they talked Jewish to us.
We understand Polish or Jewish, yeah.
We talk between me and my brothers, we talk Polish.
Speak Polish, mostly.
Yeah.
This is assimilation by-- by-- not by choice, not by-- we have no choice because we live in Poland.
We must know the language.
Yeah.
About what?
Oh yeah, well, we-- all this holidays, special Jewish holidays was celebrating very high in our-- in our home.
Pesach and-- and The New Year and Shavuot, and-- and Yom Kippur and this all kind, it was 100%.
My father was an observant Jew.
Not me.
I wasn't observant, you know.
My mother was doing this, what my father do.
[LAUGHS]
You know?
A family.
So no, before, because we was educated in Polish school, we got contact with Poles and Polish friends.
Then it was easy for us to converse in Polish.
And the Polish culture take us over, because where we lived.
Did your father-- he had a hardware store?
Yeah.
Saturday he was open.
People was working.
He don't work Saturday, no no.
No.
No, no.
He didn't work.
Not special, because I was-- I got a job, working some other place.
He got people working.
I don't know, he don't need me, no.
When that-- when this was happening, all the-- the antisemitic acts--
Yeah.
Yeah, we speak, always, we can-- we can-- we-- we can't solve the situation, we can just speak about this.
There's no-- no way out.
No way out.
That's all.
We can speak.
Because we tried to have some maybe visas to United State or something, you know, it doesn't go through.
Because there was a special quota for Polish people.
And you wait for a visa, you can wait 100 years before they can go, have a visa.
Sometimes, by good luck.
Yes, we tried, and this doesn't help.
It's affected this way-- they got a treaty, in fact.
The Polacks make a treaty with Germany.
And this treaty, they make all kind-- I mean, it's got commerce and something like this.
No, the Germans got a plan.
They got a treaty.
And they got a plan.
So this way they give it some money under the table to this [INAUDIBLE], [POLISH].
And they do the job against the Jews.
This way boiling up-- there's more boiling.
Well, not the war.
When they-- when the war don't start in September 1st, '39, there were big pogroms in Poland.
The situation start to be very-- very dangerous for the Jews.
Well, in life I belonged to organization.
I was thinking, [INAUDIBLE] some day I go to Israel and-- and fight for Israel.
We was prepared.
We got the organization goal, call this Brit HaHayal.
Every Jew-- not every Jew.
Jewish people, what they finish the Polish service in the Polish army who want belong to our organization, the Brit HaHayal.
This mean like, veterans here.
You know, it's not veterans.
They're people, what finish the army.
They know how to handle arms.
And some day we think the situation was be better, we be prepared morally, physically, and can handle arms, in case when we need it.
This was the idea of our leader.
The leader was Ze'ev Jobtinsky.
And you hear about the name of Ze'ev Jobtinsky.
And Ze'ev Jobtinsky was-- I remember when I was young he was in Poland.
It was in 1938.
And it was in [INAUDIBLE].
It was a very big-- maybe it was like people coming to hear him in the thousands.
There was-- was a couple thousand people.
And I remember like like today he talk in Polish because he was a polyglot.
The man knows about 10 languages or more.
And he start in Polish.
And then he change to Jewish.
He talk in Yiddish.
And the end of his word was [SPEAKING YIDDISH].
[SPEAKING YIDDISH]-- I-- this he talk in Jewish.
This premonition in English, [YIDDISH], a catastrophe in Europe of the Yiddish-- of the Yiddish folk, [YIDDISH].
This was the end of his speech.
Nobody believe it can happen, and it happen a year-- year later.
That's--
Yeah.
[YIDDISH], [INAUDIBLE], [YIDDISH].
He got a premonition.
He feel it.
And he--
I was there when he talked.
This was-- that's why--
This was the meeting in [? circus. ?] In Warsaw [?
Circus. ?] In [? circus ?] can go in about 6,000, 7,000 people in [? circus. ?] It was a lot of people.
They hear him and know what they can do about this.
The borders was closed.
And a year later, we'll start the war, the Second World War.
And then Hitler came.
The whole treaties with Poland was worth a piece of paper [INAUDIBLE], excuse me.
And this what happened.
And that's how the Jewish people was in big trouble.
That's-- I was lucky.
I was in the army.
Yeah, I got a piece of paper, called to the army.
And I stay-- I went to the place where's I supposed to be.
Huh?
This is the Polish Army.
That's the one.
I went over there.
We stay over there in Szczecin, some other Polish cities.
And--
Szczecin.
It's a Polish border-- not borders, it's in the-- it's-- it's-- it's in the west-- I mean, in West Poland and border of Germany.
So and this time they start--
Yeah.
And this time start with small things, start with like a-- a border, you know, on the border start shooting and this, and this.
They check and they check.
No there-- there was not official war, nothing.
And--
This was the end of '38.
In '39, there was this.
Oh, as a Jew, you feel-- you feel you're discriminated.
Because you can-- you can't advance in the Polish Army.
You can advance maybe one-- one stripe or two stripes.
And they got officers in Polish Army because they was doctors.
The doctors, they must give them a special, like a Captain or something, because he's a doctor.
Doctors or some other specialist-- highly-specialized people, mostly from Galicia.
Because Galicia belonged before the war, the First World War, belonged to Austria.
They got more privileges.
And these people over there got more education.
And they got over there, in this-- in this unions, they got some officers what's-- Jews what's officers.
Not so high office, and officers.
So--
In the army?
Not special--
The training is a regular training, like a soldier go through.
You see, it was running and shooting.
And going and working.
Yeah.
And they're very, very hard.
Because for a Jew, they discriminate you.
They want to-- sometime you hear you're-- you're [INAUDIBLE] or something, you know?
It's you know what I mean, a-- a-- a Jewish guy, a Jew.
I mean, it's not a Jew.
You're less like a Jew.
it's like an insult, like a [NON-ENGLISH].
[LAUGHS] [NON-ENGLISH] is, I mean, is not a full Jew, it's a small Jew, a [NON-ENGLISH]
So that's-- a little Jew.
Well, that's--
[NON-ENGLISH] is-- is-- they got to tell you, I mean, it's something, they won't tell you something, you have [NON-ENGLISH].
It mean you're a Jew or something like this.
[LAUGHS] It's a special explanation, Polish.
You can't explain-- [LAUGHS] You can't translate, the [POLISH] to Jewish, in Polish.
So that's so how-- anyway, in 1939, they first start the war again.
That start the regular war.
And then start the big trouble.
The 1st of September, I was in Warsaw at this time.
And the army, our army--
--the Polish Army.
In Warsaw.
No, no, no.
We was in the-- in the units, so army units.
Some there--
The army just-- those stationed there.
Stationed there or something.
And the city was bombarded day and night for two weeks, without end.
So the end-- this, the army, when they can go out they go out.
And I was on the-- I went out with the-- with the unit, what they call is-- in German was a [GERMAN].
This is armored-- armored trains.
And then they tell all soldiers what they can take it.
They think they form a new army.
Because the army was destroyed in the West.
They were forming a new army on the Bug.
The Bug is in East Poland.
And in this go from north to down.
And over there they think they go back over there they can form.
Because they got reserves, can make a new Polish Army.
I don't know this.
I don't remember The division was-- was a mixture of all-- all soldiers what runaway.
They what formed from the soldiers than run away from all the places to form.
Because the train was loaded with ammunitions.
And it was connected with a big train loaded with armour, with artillery, with-- loaded with everything, what we can remember, what you can take it out.
And when this train goes and it was attacked by the German Stukas.
Yeah.
Yeah.
